questions.question: Which JavaScript keyword declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned?
questions.explanation: `let` creates a block-scoped binding and allows reassignment later.

questions.question: Which array method creates a new array by transforming every item in the original array?
questions.explanation: `map()` returns a new array containing the transformed values for each element.

questions.question: Which built-in function converts a valid JSON string into a JavaScript object?
questions.explanation: `JSON.parse()` reads JSON text and returns the corresponding JavaScript value.

questions.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function bundled with references to its lexical environment
A special loop that closes open files
A syntax rule for ending statements with semicolons
A method used to cancel promises
questions.answer: A function bundled with references to its lexical environment
questions.explanation: Closures let functions remember variables from the scope where they were created.

questions.question: Why is event delegation useful?
options: It lets one parent listener handle events from many child elements
It automatically prevents default browser behavior
It makes all event handlers synchronous
It disables bubbling for nested elements
questions.answer: It lets one parent listener handle events from many child elements
questions.explanation: Event delegation reduces listeners and works well for dynamic lists by relying on bubbling.

questions.question: What does an `async` function always return?
questions.explanation: An `async` function wraps its returned value in a Promise automatically.

questions.question: When both a resolved Promise callback and `setTimeout(fn, 0)` are queued, which runs first?
questions.explanation: Promise callbacks are microtasks, and microtasks run before the next macrotask such as `setTimeout`.

questions.question: If an object does not contain a property directly, where does JavaScript look next during property access?
questions.explanation: Property lookup moves up the prototype chain until the property is found or the chain ends.

questions.question: Which technique is best suited to delay a function until a burst of repeated events has stopped?
questions.explanation: Debouncing waits for the event stream to pause before running the function.

questions.question: Which HTML element is most appropriate for the main heading area at the top of a page?
questions.explanation: `<header>` is the semantic container for introductory content and headings.

questions.question: Which CSS declaration enables Flexbox layout on a container?
questions.explanation: Flexbox starts when an element is given `display: flex` or `display: inline-flex`.

questions.question: Which selector targets elements with the class name `card`?
questions.explanation: A period before the name selects elements by class in CSS.

questions.question: What does `grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);` create?
options: One row with three pixel-based columns
A single column that repeats content three times
questions.explanation: `repeat(3, 1fr)` makes three columns that each take one equal fraction of the space.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of `aria-label` in HTML?
options: To provide an accessible name for assistive technologies
To apply a reusable CSS class
questions.answer: To provide an accessible name for assistive technologies
questions.explanation: `aria-label` gives screen readers a text label when visible text is missing or insufficient.

questions.question: What behavior does `position: sticky; top: 0;` usually produce?
options: The element scrolls normally until it reaches the top, then sticks there
The element is permanently fixed to the viewport from page load
The element becomes hidden behind the body
The element is removed from document flow entirely
questions.answer: The element scrolls normally until it reaches the top, then sticks there
questions.explanation: Sticky positioning acts relative at first, then behaves like fixed when its threshold is reached.

questions.question: Which selector has the highest specificity?
questions.explanation: An ID selector outweighs class and element selectors in CSS specificity.

questions.question: Why is adding the `defer` attribute to a script often useful?
options: It delays script execution until after HTML parsing is complete
It converts the script into a CSS resource
It guarantees the script runs before the document starts parsing
It automatically minifies the script file
questions.answer: It delays script execution until after HTML parsing is complete
questions.explanation: `defer` helps avoid blocking parsing and runs scripts after the document has been parsed.

questions.question: Which CSS property is commonly used to keep an image covering its container without distortion?
questions.explanation: `object-fit: cover` scales replaced elements like images to fill their box while preserving aspect ratio.

questions.question: What is the result of `len("Quiz")` in Python?
questions.explanation: The string `Quiz` contains four characters, so `len()` returns 4.

questions.question: Which data type is mutable in Python?
questions.explanation: Lists can be modified in place by adding, removing, or replacing elements.

questions.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
questions.explanation: Regular named functions are introduced with the `def` keyword.

questions.question: What does the `yield` keyword do inside a function?
options: Turns the function into a generator
Stops all loops in the program
questions.answer: Turns the function into a generator
questions.explanation: `yield` lets the function produce values one at a time while preserving its state.

questions.question: What is the difference between `sorted(items)` and `items.sort()`?
options: `sorted(items)` returns a new list, while `items.sort()` sorts the list in place
`items.sort()` returns a new list, while `sorted(items)` mutates the original
They are identical in every way
questions.answer: `sorted(items)` returns a new list, while `items.sort()` sorts the list in place
questions.explanation: `sorted()` leaves the original list untouched, but `.sort()` modifies the existing list.

questions.question: What is `**kwargs` typically used for?
options: Collecting variable keyword arguments into a dictionary
Forcing all arguments to be positional only
Declaring private variables inside a class
Creating a tuple of unnamed return values
questions.answer: Collecting variable keyword arguments into a dictionary
questions.explanation: `**kwargs` gathers extra named arguments so functions can accept flexible keyword inputs.

questions.question: Why can using a mutable object like `[]` as a default function argument be risky?
options: The same object is reused across calls
Mutable defaults are slower than loops
Python refuses to compile the function
It automatically creates a deep copy each time
questions.answer: The same object is reused across calls
questions.explanation: Default arguments are evaluated once when the function is defined, not each time it runs.

questions.question: What does the `with` statement guarantee when using a context manager?
options: Cleanup code runs when the block exits
The code inside the block becomes asynchronous
questions.answer: Cleanup code runs when the block exits
questions.explanation: Context managers ensure setup and cleanup happen reliably, even if an error occurs.

questions.question: What is true about a coroutine created by calling an `async def` function in Python?
options: It must be awaited or scheduled to run
It runs immediately like a normal function
It always returns a thread object
It can only be used inside a class
questions.answer: It must be awaited or scheduled to run
questions.explanation: Calling an `async def` function creates a coroutine object, which does not execute until awaited or scheduled.

questions.question: Which data structure follows the Last In, First Out principle?
questions.explanation: A stack removes the most recently added item first.

questions.question: What prerequisite does binary search require?
options: The data must contain unique values
The array must have an odd length
The input must be a linked list
questions.explanation: Binary search works by repeatedly halving an ordered search space.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of linear search in the worst case?
questions.explanation: Linear search may inspect every element, so its worst-case runtime grows proportionally with n.

questions.question: Which traversal is commonly used to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
questions.explanation: Breadth-first search explores layer by layer, so the first time it reaches a node is via the shortest unweighted path.

questions.question: What is the average-case time complexity of hash table lookup?
questions.explanation: With a good hash function and controlled collisions, average lookup is constant time.

questions.question: What does a min-heap guarantee?
options: The smallest element is at the root
Each node has exactly two children
questions.answer: The smallest element is at the root
questions.explanation: A min-heap only guarantees parent nodes are less than or equal to their children.

questions.question: Why does Dijkstra's algorithm fail on graphs with negative edge weights?
options: A later negative edge can produce a shorter path after a node was finalized
Negative numbers cannot be stored in adjacency lists
The algorithm only works on trees
questions.answer: A later negative edge can produce a shorter path after a node was finalized
questions.explanation: Dijkstra assumes once the shortest node is chosen, no cheaper path will appear later, which negative edges break.

questions.question: Topological sorting is defined only for which kind of graph?
questions.explanation: Topological order requires directed edges and no cycles, otherwise a valid ordering cannot exist.

questions.question: Which property makes an AVL tree self-balancing?
options: The height difference between left and right subtrees stays within 1
Every node stores at least two keys
All leaves remain on the same level
questions.answer: The height difference between left and right subtrees stays within 1
questions.explanation: AVL trees maintain a balance factor so operations remain logarithmic.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Japan?
questions.explanation: Tokyo is the capital and the largest metropolitan area in Japan.

questions.question: Which planet is commonly called the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars appears reddish because of iron oxide on its surface.

questions.question: Which ocean is the largest on Earth?
questions.explanation: The Pacific Ocean covers more area than any other ocean on Earth.

questions.question: Which gas makes up most of Earth's atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen makes up roughly 78 percent of Earth's atmosphere.

questions.question: In which city is the headquarters of the World Health Organization located?
questions.explanation: The World Health Organization is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

questions.question: Which scientist proposed the three laws of motion?
questions.explanation: Newton's laws of motion are foundational principles in classical mechanics.

questions.question: Which treaty formally ended World War I?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles, signed in 1919, formally ended the state of war between Germany and the Allied Powers.

questions.question: Which layer of Earth is liquid and lies between the mantle and the inner core?
questions.explanation: The outer core is a liquid layer of iron and nickel surrounding the inner core.

questions.question: Which planet has the shortest day in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter rotates faster than the other planets, completing a day in about 10 hours.
